speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Liberator Mr. James Zuidema in Muskegon, Michigan, December the 1st, 1996. And this is tape 4. So they sent you home. Do you remember what month that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: June. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: June.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came home-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got home just about-- I was discharged on June 30, 1946, at 1 o'clock, having gone in on June 28. So it was two hours, two days-- no, two years, two hours-- two days, and two hours I was in the Army. And came home-- my brother was in the seminary and working at the dairy during the summer. And he wanted to take a couple weeks off. So I had lived at the dairy practically my whole life. My dad worked there. My brother asked the boss if I could take his place for a couple weeks. And Dave says sure, Jimmy can come in. No problem. So I stayed five and a half years. [LAUGHTER] I used to tell him they couldn't fire me because he never hired me. [LAUGHTER] Bill came back, and they never told me I was done. So I just stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to family and friends back in Muskegon about what you had seen at Ohrdruf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much. Not very much. I just mentioned it. Before my mother died, when it came out in the paper a couple years ago, she, she didn't even remember me ever mentioning it.

speaker: interviewer
text: To people that you mentioned it, do you recall how they reacted to what they heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean before? Or, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Immediately when you came back. When you spoke to family or friends about it, do you remember how they reacted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kind of blase, more-- oh, that was nice that you, you were there, something like that, more or less. I-- by that time there was so much about it, you know, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] so many camps. The, the British had gone in Bergen-Belsen towards the end of April. Buchenwald was taken April 11th. That was almost in a direct line east of Ohrdruf, probably 40, 50 miles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nordhausen, just north of Ohrdruf was taken. There were camps-- oh, there were hundreds of camps. I think in Germany, there were over 300 or 400 camps all over. In fact, anywhere you went, there was a camp of some sort. So they, they were coming fast, and so on. You might say they flooded the market with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] experiences of stuff like that. So it became more everyday occurrence when people say, well, I was there. I was there. Or I saw this. I saw that, you know. I don't remember anybody really getting upset about it until just the last five or six years that things had been coming out more than what it did before.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the experience impact you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, up until just a few years ago-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a while there, I, I almost got to the point when I didn't think that Ohrdruf really existed. I knew I had it in my photo album-- Ohrdruf-- and on my, my paper that we wrote up after the war ended, more or less like an itinerary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But when you hear all about all the other camps-- and we never heard anything about Ohrdruf. And then one-- oh, it must have been-- I don't know how long ago-- 10, 15, maybe more and that. I was reading in I believe it was the book, And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right at the end, there was just one line that said that he had entered Ohrdruf. And I, I looked at it. And I thought, my god, I wasn't dreaming after all. I mean, here, here it says it in, in writing, 'Ohrdruf.' So I wasn't imagining that-- not imagining that I saw this, but imagining that was the wrong name for it, or something. Well then, a couple of years later, I was watching television and watching the movie And then when I got to that point, there was about three or four minutes-- it was quite extensive, where in the book, it was just one line. In the movie, it, it, it showed a lot. And they mixed actual footage taken that day with Hollywood footage and then colorized the black and white, the old stuff. And I remember I was in the back room watching the television. And the kids were in, in their rooms. And I don't know where Theresa was. And I, I just let a whoop out. I mean, I just went crazy. And they came out. I said, there it is. There it is, you know. And then finally, I did-- eventually get the tape and copied it. So I have a copy of it. But up to that time-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was the first that we-- that I've heard Ohrdruf mentioned, actually mentioned, other than what I've remembered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was-- it was-- it was kind of a relief, you know, that I wasn't-- that I had the right name, that what I-- what I saw I had placed in the right place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went through a rather long period of time after the war that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it, it wasn't a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a big issue in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then, just a couple years ago, I was outdoors with the kids. We have a daycare. And I'm, I'm always outdoors in the afternoon during the summer with the kids. And I came in to go to the bathroom. And Theresa went out to take my place. We switched places. I came in. The television was on. Now it's-- at that time of day, there was no kids in the house. It was on We'd never watch that program, never. I, I hate him. The people on there are stupid. And this one-- this day, they had a guy on it that was yelling and screaming that the Holocaust was all a fake. It was all a big propaganda thing. And there was nothing to it. And I, I just-- I just stood there in shock. And I would-- and I, I, I was so mad, I started screaming at the television. You stupid-- you know, bad, bad words. [LAUGHTER] And I was just going hysterical almost, screaming at it.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can-- how can you say such stupid things? I I saw it, too. And people were pulling up their sleeves, and saying, there's my number. How can you say this didn't happen? And, you know, oh, I, I-- then finally, Theresa came in. And, and she said, what's the matter? I said, look, look at this SOB, what he's saying. And she says, you know, calm down. You're going to have another heart attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, I'm going to write 'The Public Sees It.' I says, this is ridiculous, how people can say such things. Well, just by coincidence, we used to take care of this editor's secretary's little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Editor of?

speaker: interviewee
text: in Muskegon. It's a local paper. And I was going to write the paper and 'The Public Sees It,' you know. And so Theresa called [? Penny. ?] And she says, Jim's on a warpath. He's sending a letter into the 'Public Sees It' editor. And [? Penny ?] says, well, just tell him to calm down. And a while back-- you know, a couple hours later, she called back and said, I've talked to the-- to the editor and the powers to be down here. And they're sending someone out to talk to him. So they sent a girl out and, and a photographer. And we sat here, like you and I are talking here, and-- I think over three hours. And [CRYING] both of us were in tears, practically [SOBBING] the whole time, because she, she had had-- I think her dad had some experience in it, too. And then a couple weeks later on Sunday morning, the whole front page-- [LAUGHS] I've got a copy of it here I was going to show you-- but-- came out. And then the telephone just went crazy. Everybody was calling. Nothing bad, you know. Nothing-- but everybody, you know, saying, you got-- you got something to say. Now, get out and say it. And I, I-- finally, I just said, I can't take any more calls. I can't talk to anybody. And people were calling. They wanted me to come and talk. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wanted you to talk to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I says, I, I-- hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wanted you to talk to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different groups. School kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What do you call it? Service organizations. I, I says, I can't. I, I, I, [SIGHS] I, I, I get-- I get all broke up talking about it. And-- because I got so shook just watching it on television there. And finally, they-- a couple-- a real close friend says, you've got something to say, say it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I finally did agree. Before I talked to my first school, I, I talked to the rabbi and, [CRYING] you know, told him what had happened. And he says, Jim, I could go say exactly what you're going to say. And they'll they'll say, you're a Jew. You're a rabbi. You've got an axe to grind. You've got a chip on your shoulder. You've got something to gain from this. He says, you're Catholic. You have nothing to gain. All you're doing is saying what you saw. He says, they'll believe you. So, [CRYING] so he said, please go for it. So I did. It was-- it was awful hard, terrible hard. The first time I talked was in Grand Haven to a middle school. The kids were a little bit young. Those were the youngest ones I've ever talked to. They-- the questions they asked afterwards-- you know, I had pictures. And I showed some of my video footage that I had taken off the television of Ohrdruf. I've collected quite a bit of it. Some of it's duplicate stuff. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] their questions weren't quite thoughtful.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then one of my friends that I sing with is an English teacher at Reeths-Puffer. He asked me to come. And I says, OK. And I says, well, when? And he says, well, my first class is at 7 o'clock, then 8 o'clock, 9 o'clock, 10 o'clock, 1 o'clock, and 2 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, oh gosh. I says, I, I got to make arrangements for that. I just can't leave Theresa all by herself with all the kids. So, so when I did do it, you know, I spent the whole day over there. And afterwards, he, he had all the kids write me a letter and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] telling what they thought. And one-- the one that I, I prize the most was from a boy. And he says the usual chitchat. chat. But he said, this is the first time I didn't fall asleep in class. [LAUGHTER] Kids were-- after the first class, kids were coming to the door that weren't in the class. They wanted to come in because then I was a very strict time, on the hour, you know. And teachers-- during the day, the teachers were coming in that didn't have a class. The principals came in and sat in on a class. It was-- it was quite a moving thing. I don't know how many times I did it that first day. But I didn't get through any of them [LAUGHS] without starting to go to pieces. But then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I've spoken to a lot of service groups. Usually they say, well, you're allotted half an hour, 40 minutes at the most. One time, it was almost two hours. And they didn't want me to stop. But I had to stop. And I just-- I just-- but then we just stood around and talked afterwards. But it was-- but those-- the older people-- the older kids and the older people are real appreciative. And they ask some real good questions, you know. What was going on, and stuff like that. They really appreciate it. Even though there's been last-- well, since-- for the 50 year-- since the war ended, there's been a lot about that stuff on television. It's, it's not a type of program that you would just sit down to watch because you want to. You know, I-- if, if anything is on about-- anything about World War II, I watch it. But-- so people are learning. And especially when you've got the actual photographs and stuff like that, I think it's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: In light of your experiences, do you have a message for future generations who might see the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what, what I-- what I-- what I told-- you can't say this to older people because it-- it's not-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, it's-- but to the kids in school, I tell them-- I says, OK, now, 50 years from now, when some jerk like there was on comes on-- comes into your room, comes into your house on this 3D television, you know, like-- looks like you're right there, and he starts telling you that this was all a hoax, and there was nothing to it, it's all fake. And you can say, well, this old man-- [CRYING] when, when we were in school, this old man came and told us and showed us pictures. Because he was there. So you can say, I know this happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a more philosophical view, I would say, learn to live with each other, for god's sakes, you know, people. It's going on today almost as bad as it did then. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans did it. The Russians did it. Now they're doing it down these other countries, killing each other. For what? Why can't people together? I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what I'd say. Learn to live together-- peace, shalom. Do you have any other thoughts that you'd like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think I've said enough. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] It's been hard. You think, after 50 years, [CRYING] you get over it. But you don't. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Maybe because I've-- I've been learning so much about it since-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the last 10, 15 years. Because over there, like I said, you don't know what's going on around you. So a lot of the stuff, the details, the history, and stuff like that you learn afterwards and then have to fit it-- fit what you saw in with what, what it-- what, what you saw. But you didn't know what you saw. And now we-- we know what we saw and can put it into history.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we don't realize [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what significance it meant at the time. But now we do. And sometimes, it makes it a little bit harder, even. If we could have retained our so-called innocence and not-- and just forgot about it. But I-- I've-- I haven't been able to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other guys haven't, too. I've talked to some of the guys that called up right after it came out in the paper. One guy says, I got some pictures. But I keep them hidden in my drawer. And I'm afraid to take them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, why? He says, well, I'm afraid someone is going to come and get me. And I says, for god's sakes, take them out and show them to your kids. His kids were all grown up and married. So he did. He called back later and he says, I showed them to my kids. They-- they couldn't believe the pictures that I had, that he had taken in, in, you know, different camps. And-- but everybody in the G.I.s-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] some of them-- we had one, one of my buddies' wives said, what's, what's-- told Theresa, what, what-- what's he so hepped up about? There were other people that saw stuff like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Theresa says, yeah, but they, they won't go out and talk-- tell people what they saw. So I think that's, that's the important thing. Not that I'm any sort of a hero or anything like that, but, it's one of the hardest things I've ever done, like right today. But if people will listen to me, I'll, I'll, I'll tell them what I saw. And I think that is-- that's the least I can do-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because so many million people did more than what I did. But they gave their lives. But at least I can tell, maybe they won't die in vain-- I don't know. I hope not. That's my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture I took the first day when we went into Ohrdruf. You just see there's no GIs standing around. Several days later, when Eisenhower came, he stood almost in this exactly same spot. And the picture that is so famous with Eisenhower, Patton, Bradley, all the GIs standing around is almost this identical picture. And when we talk to groups-- when I talk to groups, I tell them, I, I focus on what they saw. And the other picture focuses on who was seeing it. In this shot-- this is the room that I think-- I'm not sure, but I think-- is the site that almost that made Patton sick to his stomach. And he had to leave. There is some debate about it. But this-- I think this is the shot. And this one looks like an old lady. But it's actually a man. If you look at the eyes, the, the blank look on the face gives you some idea-- even though he has clothes on and that rag tied around his head-- the expressions that we experienced of the survivors at Ohrdruf. This is a portrait drawn of me right after the war ended in Bad Schallerbach, Austria, by a German POW. You see, I was very young, just 19 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?
